content.problemStatement:
We will implement Prim's algorithm using Priority Queue. The queue will help us to get the minimum weight edge every time.

Select any vertex, say 1 and push all the edges from 1 into the queue. 

Perform the following operations while queue is not empty.


Take the minimum weight edge from the queue.
If the edge connects a vertex that is already in the tree then skip this edge.
Otherwise perform the following operations.

Add the edge to the Minimum Spanning Tree.
Push all the edges that goes from the newly connected vertex.


TaskPrim's algorithm is implemented in the IDE. 

The following graph is processed using the algorithm; run the code and check the output.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two integers n and m — the number of vertices and edges. The next m lines each contain three integers u, v, w describing an undirected edge between vertices u and v with weight w.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output the total weight of the Minimum Spanning Tree on a single line, followed by (n‑1) lines each containing two integers u and v representing an edge of the MST. The edges can be printed in the order they are selected by Prim's algorithm.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The MST consists of edges with total weight 14 as shown.
The MST weight is 7, obtained by edges (1,2), (2,3) and (2,4).

solution.approach: We run Prim's algorithm starting from vertex 1. A min‑heap (priority queue) always gives the smallest weight edge that connects the current tree to an unvisited vertex. When a vertex is added, all its incident edges to still unvisited vertices are pushed into the heap. The process stops after n‑1 edges have been selected. The sum of the selected edge weights is the MST weight. The algorithm is optimal because Prim's greedy choice property guarantees that the locally cheapest edge extending the tree never prevents an optimal solution.
solution.timeComplexity: O((n + m) log n) per test case – each edge is inserted into the priority queue at most once and each extraction costs log n.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n + m) for the adjacency lists, visited array and the priority queue.
